THE ULTIMATE

EVENT PLAYBOOK



Claude × Anthropic Community Workshop
Hosted at Even House • Tustin, CA
15771 Red Hill Ave, Suite 500, Tustin, CA 92780

Prepared by Travis Boomgaarden
AuraPath AI • OCPM • FaithTech OC

1. Venue Intel: Even House
Even House is a premium members-only club at 15771 Red Hill Ave, Suite 500, Tustin, CA 92780. It features coworking space, an organic kitchen, coffee bar, beer on tap, private meeting rooms, an outdoor patio, Trackman golf simulators, and a putting course. The vibe is upscale but approachable—think Soho House meets golf culture.
Key Logistics to Confirm with Even House
Hours: Tue–Thu 8:30am–8pm, Fri–Sat 8:30am–10pm, Sun 8:30am–6pm. CLOSED MONDAYS.
Guest policy: Members can bring up to 3 guests at a time. For larger groups, you MUST contact their Concierge to arrange a private event. Non-members need an approved day pass or registered guest check-in.
WiFi: Fast WiFi throughout—confirm bandwidth can handle 20+ simultaneous laptop users running Claude.ai.
A/V setup: Ask about projector/screen or large TV availability in the meeting room, plus any HDMI/USB-C adapters needed.
Food & beverage: Their organic kitchen serves seed-oil-free, wholesome food. Their in-house coffee bar and beer on tap are included in the space. Discuss whether you can pre-order a catering package or if attendees order individually.
Parking: Free parking available right outside the building—include this in all communications.
Capacity: Confirm max headcount for the meeting room vs. open lounge area for your workshop format.
Club is NOT pet-friendly. Mention in communications if relevant.

⚠️  ACTION ITEM
Contact Even House Concierge at (949) 545-5855 or info@evenhouse.club at least 3–4 weeks before the event. Provide: date, expected headcount, AV needs, food/beverage requirements, and power strip needs.


2. The Ritz-Carlton Hospitality Framework
The Ritz-Carlton’s Gold Standards are the benchmark for world-class experience. Here’s how to adapt their three core service steps to your workshop:
Three Steps of Service, Adapted
STEP 1
Warm Welcome
Greet every single person by name at the door. Use name tags with first names in large print. Have a dedicated greeter (you or a volunteer) whose only job for the first 15 minutes is making people feel seen. Offer a warm beverage, show them where things are, and personally introduce them to at least one other person.


STEP 2
Anticipate Needs
Before anyone asks: have power strips pre-placed at every table, WiFi credentials displayed prominently on table tents, water bottles at each seat, and a printed quick-start guide. During hands-on time, circulate proactively—don’t wait for raised hands. Watch for confused faces and approach gently.


STEP 3
Fond Farewell
End with energy, not a fizzle. Thank every person individually. Give them a tangible takeaway: a printed resource card, a link to a community Slack/Discord, or a follow-up email template they can use immediately. Walk people to the door. Follow up within 48 hours with session notes, slides, and a personal thank-you.


3. Before the Event: Ensuring 90%+ Show Rate
No-shows are the silent killer of community events. Here is a proven multi-touch communication strategy:
Communication Timeline
When
Channel
What to Communicate
2–3 weeks out
Email + LinkedIn
Save-the-date with venue photo, parking info (free!), what to bring (laptop + charger), what they’ll build. Set the tone: “This is hands-on. You WILL build something.”
1 week out
Email + SMS
Logistics reminder: address (15771 Red Hill Ave, Ste 500), parking, arrive 10 min early. Include a “prep task”—e.g., create a free Claude.ai account. This creates investment.
2 days before
Personal text or DM
Personal message: “Hey [Name], really looking forward to seeing you Thursday! Quick reminder to bring your laptop. Any questions?” Personal > mass every time.
Morning of
SMS or WhatsApp
Quick energy message: “Today’s the day! Coffee will be ready. See you at [time]. Parking is right outside the building.”


Psychological Attendance Drivers
Create social commitment: Ask registrants to reply “I’m in!” or post publicly they’re attending. Public commitment = higher follow-through.
Pre-assign “roles”: Tell someone they’ll be your “wifi helper” or ask them to help welcome newcomers. People who have a role always show up.
Scarcity framing: “We’re capping at 20 spots to keep it intimate.” Even if you have room for 30, a smaller cap signals exclusivity.
Prep task investment: Asking people to pre-create their Claude account means they’ve already invested 5 minutes. Sunk cost drives attendance.
Buddy system: Encourage registrants to bring a friend or colleague. Social accountability is powerful.

Pre-Event Checklist
Confirm reservation and AV with Even House Concierge
Test WiFi bandwidth with multiple devices running Claude.ai simultaneously
Print name tags (first name LARGE, company small)
Print WiFi credentials on table tents (one per table)
Prepare power strips / extension cords (one per table cluster)
Print quick-start guides / cheat sheets for hands-on exercises
Prepare a slide deck with clear timing visible to attendees
Set up feedback form (Google Form or Tally) with QR code
Prepare takeaway resource card (links, communities, next steps)
Coordinate food/beverage with Even House kitchen
Charge all devices, test screen mirroring / HDMI adapter
Prepare a Spotify playlist for arrival and break times

4. Workshop Time Structure (Recommended: 2.5–3 Hours)
The golden ratio for workshops is roughly 30% teaching / 50% hands-on / 20% connection. Never lecture for more than 15 minutes without an interaction point.
Time
Duration
Activity & Notes
0:00
15 min
ARRIVAL & CONNECTION — Music playing, coffee ready, name tags at door. Greet every person by name. Introduce newcomers to each other. Never let anyone stand alone.
0:15
10 min
WELCOME & CONTEXT — Brief intro: who you are, why this matters, what they’ll walk away with. Quick show of hands: “Who has used Claude before? Who’s brand new?” This calibrates your teaching.
0:25
5 min
ICEBREAKER — Table introductions: name, what you do, one thing you’d automate if you could. Keep it tight and energizing.
0:30
15 min
TEACH BLOCK 1 — Core concept demo (e.g., “What Claude can do”). Live demo, not slides. Show real use cases that match the audience’s work. Keep energy high.
0:45
20 min
HANDS-ON 1 — Guided exercise. Everyone follows along on their own laptop. Have a printed step-by-step guide. Walk the room. Pair struggling people with confident ones.
1:05
5 min
SHARE WINS — 2–3 volunteers share what they built/discovered. Celebrate loudly. This builds momentum.
1:10
10 min
BREAK — Refresh beverages, stretch, mingle. Play music. This is networking time disguised as a break.
1:20
15 min
TEACH BLOCK 2 — Advanced concept or second use case (e.g., vibe coding with Claude, building with artifacts). Again: live demo > slides.
1:35
30 min
HANDS-ON 2: BUILD SESSION — Open build time. Give a prompt: “Build something useful for YOUR work in the next 30 minutes.” Circulate aggressively. Help the stuck. Challenge the advanced.
2:05
15 min
SHOWCASE & CELEBRATE — 5–6 people demo what they built (2–3 min each). Cheer for everyone. This is the emotional peak of the event.
2:20
10 min
WRAP & NEXT STEPS — Recap key takeaways. Share resource links, community channels, next event date. Ask them to fill out feedback form (QR code on screen). Give your fond farewell.
2:30
15+ min
OPEN NETWORKING — Don’t rush people out. Let connections happen naturally. This is where the real magic lives. Stay until the last person leaves.


5. Teaching Content: The “Vibe Coding” Approach
Your audience will likely span absolute beginners to experienced developers. The vibe coding framework you’ve developed is perfect because it meets both ends. Here’s how to structure the content:
Content Principles
Show, don’t tell. Live demos beat slides 10:1. Open Claude.ai and BUILD something in front of them.
Use real-world problems. Don’t demo “write me a haiku.” Demo: “Let me draft a client proposal in 90 seconds.” Match problems to the room.
Layer complexity. Start with simple prompting, then show artifacts, then show building a mini-app. Each layer should feel like a superpower unlock.
Browser-only tools. Stick to Claude.ai, v0.dev, and other browser-based tools. No local dev environments. This keeps beginners from getting lost in setup.
Fail forward. When a demo doesn’t work perfectly, narrate your debugging. This is MORE valuable than a perfect demo because it teaches the real skill.
Name the magic. When something amazing happens, pause and say: “Did you see that? You just built [thing] in 3 minutes. That used to take a developer 3 hours.” Make them feel the power.
Hands-On Exercise Ideas
Guided: Everyone prompts Claude to draft an email, then iterates on it using follow-up instructions. Teaches the conversation pattern.
Intermediate: Use Claude to build a simple landing page or internal tool using artifacts. Shows the “vibe coding” concept live.
Advanced: Open build challenge — “Build something useful for your work in 30 minutes.” Share at the end.
Bonus for healthcare/non-tech audiences: Use Claude to create patient education materials, summarize research papers, or draft SOPs.
Managing Mixed Skill Levels
Use the “floor and ceiling” method: give everyone the same starting exercise (the floor), but have stretch challenges ready for advanced users (the ceiling). Print a “Bonus Challenges” sheet for people who finish early. Pair beginners with experienced users during build time—this benefits both.

6. Making Every Person Feel Valued
This is the Ritz-Carlton differentiator. Most workshops treat attendees as an audience. You’re treating them as honored guests. Here’s the detail that separates good from unforgettable:
Before They Arrive
Send a personal welcome message (not mass email) referencing something specific about them: their company, their LinkedIn post, why you’re glad they’re coming.
Ask about dietary restrictions or accessibility needs in your RSVP form. Acting on this tells people you see them as individuals.
Create a shared Google Doc or Notion page where attendees can introduce themselves before the event. This warms the room before anyone walks in.
During the Event
Greet by name. This is non-negotiable. Print your RSVP list and study it. When someone walks in and you say “Hey Sarah, so glad you’re here!”—that changes everything.
Make introductions. Don’t let anyone stand alone. Your job is to be the connector: “Have you met [Name]? They work in [industry] too—you two should chat.”
Acknowledge contributions. When someone shares in a discussion or demos their work, celebrate them publicly. Use their name. “That’s amazing, Marcus—you just built a client intake form in 8 minutes.”
Check in with quiet people. Walk to their table during hands-on time. Ask: “How are you doing? What are you working on?” Don’t call on introverts publicly—approach them 1-on-1.
Never make anyone feel stupid. If someone asks a “basic” question, respond with: “That’s a great question, and I’m glad you asked because I bet others are wondering the same thing.”
Create micro-moments of delight. A hand-written name on their seat. A small branded sticker. A printed “your first prompt” card at their spot. These tiny touches communicate care.
After the Event
Send a personal follow-up within 48 hours. Reference something specific they said or built: “Hey David, that email automation you built was sharp—have you tried expanding it to handle replies?”
Share photos from the event. Tag people (with permission). This extends the experience and creates social proof for future events.
Invite them into your ongoing community: OCPM, a dedicated Slack channel, or a recurring meetup series.
Send a “30-day check-in”: “Have you used what you learned? What’s working? What questions came up?” This shows you care about their outcome, not just filling seats.

7. Day-Of Operations Checklist
60 Minutes Before Doors Open
Arrive early. Set up tables in clusters of 4–6 (collaboration > rows). Theater seating kills engagement.
Test ALL tech: projector/screen, WiFi, your demo on Claude.ai, screen share, audio.
Place at each seat: name tag, quick-start guide, pen, water bottle, WiFi card.
Set up power strips at every table. Test that outlets work.
Queue up your Spotify arrival playlist. Upbeat but not distracting.
Brief any volunteers or co-facilitators on their roles.
Put the feedback QR code on a slide, and print it on table tents.
Take a deep breath. You’re ready.
During the Event
Station yourself at the door for the first 15 minutes. Nothing else matters more than the greeting.
Watch the energy. If it dips during a teaching block, pivot to interaction: “Turn to your neighbor and try this.”
Keep time visible. Have a projected timer or announce: “You have 10 more minutes for this exercise.”
Take photos (or designate a photographer). Candid shots of people building, collaborating, presenting.
Have a “parking lot” for questions that go off-topic: “Great question—let’s capture that and I’ll address it at the end.”
After Close
Don’t rush cleanup. Stay present for lingering conversations.
Collect feedback forms. If people didn’t fill them out, send the link that evening.
Debrief with any co-facilitators: what worked, what to change.
Send thank-you to Even House staff.
Back up all photos and notes.

8. Common Pitfalls to Avoid
✖  Don’t
✔  Do Instead
Lecture for 30+ minutes straight
Cap teaching blocks at 15 min, then interact
Assume everyone is at the same level
Survey skill levels upfront; use floor/ceiling exercises
Let people struggle silently
Circulate proactively during hands-on time
Call on people randomly
Invite volunteers; approach introverts privately
Skip the greeting and jump to content
First 15 min = connection only. Content can wait.
End abruptly: “Okay, that’s it!”
End with celebration, next steps, and personal goodbyes
Rely solely on WiFi for demos
Have offline backups / screenshots of key steps
Forget to follow up
48-hour follow-up email with notes + personal touch


9. Measuring Success
Success isn’t just headcount. Here are the KPIs that matter for a community workshop:
Metric
Target
How to Measure
Show rate
85%+ of registrants
Registration vs. sign-in sheet
Engagement
80%+ hands-on participation
Observation + completed exercises
Satisfaction (NPS)
NPS 50+
Post-event survey
Community conversion
50%+ join Slack/Discord
Channel join count within 1 week
30-day adoption
40%+ still using Claude
30-day follow-up survey
Referral intent
“Would bring a friend” 80%+
Post-event survey question


10. Your Event Motto
“Technology should amplify human resilience, not replace it.”
Every person who walks through that door should leave feeling more capable, more connected, and more excited about what they can build.


You’re not just hosting a workshop. You’re creating a moment where someone realizes they can do things they never thought possible. That’s the Ritz-Carlton of events. That’s what people remember.

Overview

Ambassador directory will be shared soon to help connect ambassadors across cities

Weekly calls planned with rotating times to accommodate global time zones

Ambassador benefits and API credits
Ambassadors receive $100 in API credits today and $100 every month going forward

Credit links will also work for meetup, workshop, and hackathon events

Dedicated Slack channel coming for referrals to fast-track people ambassadors recommend

New ambassador landing page launches next week for directing interested people to the program

New collectible merch tiers unlocking that will only be available at community events

Additional rewards unlock as ambassadors host more meetups and grow their communities

Resource Hub video library
Otto manages content and resources for ambassador program

Resource Hub is a library of short-form video content (1-3 minutes each) to help ambassadors confidently teach and demo Claude products

Videos are modular, up to date, and event-ready—ambassadors can present them directly, remix them, or watch for personal learning

Three content types:

Baseline: Basic information about product features and capabilities

Spotlight: Deeper dives with best practices, techniques, and tips to maximize features

Starter packs: Pre-packaged content curated for specific event types (Claude Co work meetups, Claude code, verticals like designers or creatives)

Content is not prescriptive—ambassadors should remix and make it their own

Otto wants to create content collaboratively based on ambassador feedback, community questions, and Q&A needs

All ambassadors invited to Google Drive and unlisted YouTube playlist, updated very frequently

Ambassadors should share feedback on what content would be most useful

Builders Council product feedback program
Exclusive product feedback program for ambassadors to get early access to features, capabilities, and models

Ambassadors provide product team feedback on whether features hit the mark or need improvement

Participation is needs-based—not every ambassador will fit every Builders Council based on experience, background, and interests

Each Builders Council is scoped to specific feature, capability, or model launch

Councils typically run for 1-2 weeks then dissolve until the next one

Anthropic will reach out to select ambassadors for each council

Event hosting requirements
Each ambassador must host 1 event per quarter

In cities with multiple ambassadors, each ambassador is individually responsible for 1 event per quarter (not shared across the group)

Ambassadors in the same city are equal in their markets and should work together

Hackathons are open to whatever makes sense for each city and community—not limited to nonprofits or local governments

Ambassadors are the experts on what matters in their cities and should design events accordingly

Program operates with shared guidelines but without restriction, similar to how Claude was built with shared principles

Hackathons and Impact Labs
Hackathons can focus on open source projects, nonprofits, local governments, or whatever fits the local community

Anthropic cannot guarantee staff attendance at every hackathon but will try to participate, especially outside major cities

Program celebrates every city, village, township, and neighborhood equally—not just focusing on big metros

Ambassadors should propose hackathon ideas and Anthropic will design them together

Insurance and legal considerations
Anthropic cannot provide legal or insurance advice

Typically, venue bookings include insurance as the venue becomes the event organizer

Requirements vary by country and market (Germany example: insurance required per event based on attendee count and location)

If ambassadors must pay for insurance, Anthropic will figure out solutions together

Attendees must be 18 or older to participate in meetups due to global liability concerns

Age restriction is in the attendee agreement and applies universally for scalability

No current solution for under-18 participants, but may be addressed in the future

Co-branding and venue partnership guidelines
Co-branding triggers brand review and is not permitted

Ambassadors cannot take Anthropic brand assets and combine them with other brands

Do not create assets like "Venue X x Anthropic"—this misrepresents the relationship

The relationship is community and ambassadors x venue, with Anthropic sponsoring the community (not partnering with the venue)

Ambassadors can use their ambassador brand and say they are sponsored by Anthropic

Ambassadors can acknowledge and thank venues for providing space without presenting it as an Anthropic partnership

Hosting events at company venues is fine—just don't present it as "Anthropic x Company"

Martine will write better co-branding guidelines and add them to the Slack handbook

Handling business inquiries and demo requests
Ambassadors receiving significant inbound from organizations wanting Anthropic account managers, demos, or business relationships

Examples: Telcos in New Zealand, universities, local governments, hundreds of demo requests

Anthropic acknowledges this is a supply and demand problem that needs infrastructure solutions

Martine committed to providing clear pathways for what Anthropic can currently help with and what they cannot

Goal is to give ambassadors proactive guidance on what they can help with versus what's outside current capacity

Anthropic is still a startup and cannot cover all use cases yet

Team will create resources showing what's readily available and a submission system (possibly using Claude) to organize incoming opportunities

This is a top priority—solutions will be posted in Slack quickly, not in three months

Ambassadors should share signals about opportunities and community interests to help inform program development

Event format options and creativity
Ambassadors can experiment with event formats beyond traditional meetups

Suggested formats: Whiteboarding nights, Claude Cafe concepts, smaller coffee shop meetups

One Australian ambassador doing "Agented Coffee"

Community suggestions: "Claude and Chai"

Ambassadors should discuss creative formats in Slack and upcoming program meetings

Virtual/online meetups are under consideration—decision expected within 2 weeks

Felipe in Colombia noted 88 people wanted to present (not just attend) at Bogota event, suggesting demand for online format to scale across Colombian cities

Community cultivation between events
Question raised about how to maintain community engagement between events

Martine emphasized ambassadors know their markets better than the Anthropic team

Ambassadors should decide which platforms work best locally and whether to standardize or keep individual approaches

Group will tackle this together over the next few weeks or month

Adam mentioned using WhatsApp community as one approach

Goal is to find solutions ambassadors can all use while respecting local preferences

Ambassador directory and connections
Ambassador directory will be created and shared with all ambassadors

Purpose: Connect ambassadors when community members travel between cities or for cross-city collaboration

Jason committed to quickly creating a spreadsheet with ambassador locations and contact info

Ambassadors can refer people to events in other cities (example: sending London contacts to Vikram or Olivier's events)

Demo content guidelines
Question raised about filtering demo submissions and third-party tool presentations

Martine's guidance: Realness is more important than forced scripts or restricted theme lists

Avoid venturing too far from Claude products (Claude, Claude Co work, Claude code, dev platform)

Ambassadors can discuss viral topics and trending tools but should anchor back to Claude ecosystem

Third-party plugins in the Claude code ecosystem are highly encouraged even if made by third parties

Spirit of the program is about Claude and Claude products, but not a hard rule against mentioning other tools

Focus on exploring what Claude tools can do and their benefits

Keep demos relevant to the community while maintaining the Claude-focused spirit

Slack channels and communication
Four Slack channels for ambassadors

Ambassadors must individually accept invites for each channel

Internal admin teams still adding ambassadors and processing approvals

Ambassadors should expect access within the next day or so

If access doesn't happen, ambassadors should notify the team

Referral submissions will go through Slack to fast-track recommended people

Future ambassador meetups and South by Southwest
Question raised about potential in-person gathering for all ambassadors

Jason's response: "Stay tuned"

Helen's cohort from Cincy AI will be in Austin during South by Southwest with people flying in from around the world

Damon is the Austin ambassador

No official Anthropic-hosted South by Southwest event confirmed, but ambassadors can organize something

Damon offered to help line up a meetup if ambassadors want to put something together

Ambassadors encouraged to connect on Slack to coordinate

Program cadence and next steps
Weekly calls planned for ambassadors with rotating times to accommodate global time zones

Ambassadors must accept Slack invites to participate in ongoing discussions

If having issues with Slack access, email the team to troubleshoot

This is just the beginning of the program with more to come

﻿

What was the main takeaway?
What questions did Participant 18 ask?
